---
title: "Introduction to Uncertainty Quantification with PyApprox"
subtitle: "PyApprox Workshop"
---

## Workshop Overview

**Date:** 2025
**Duration:** 4 hours

This workshop provides a practical introduction to Uncertainty Quantification (UQ)
using the pyapprox library. Participants will learn core UQ concepts through hands-on
examples with an ecological population dynamics model (Lotka-Volterra 3-species).

By the end of this workshop, you will be able to:

- Frame engineering/scientific problems as UQ problems
- Propagate uncertainty through computational models
- Identify the most important parameters using sensitivity analysis
- Infer model parameters from observed data


## Prerequisites

- Python programming experience (NumPy familiarity helpful)
- Basic probability and statistics (mean, variance, distributions)
- Basic calculus (derivatives, integrals)
- Laptop with pyapprox installed (see setup tutorial)

## Schedule

| Time | Topic | Duration |
|------|-------|----------|
| +0 min | [Setup Environment](../../library/setup_environment.qmd) | 15 min |
| +15 min | [Uq Problem Framing](../../library/uq_problem_framing.qmd) | 25 min |
| +40 min | [Forward Model Definition](../../library/forward_model_definition.qmd) | 25 min |
| +1:05 | [Random Variables Distributions](../../library/random_variables_distributions.qmd) | 25 min |
| +1:30 | **Break** | 15 min |
| +1:45 | [Quantities Of Interest](../../library/quantities_of_interest.qmd) | 20 min |
| +2:05 | [Monte Carlo Basics](../../library/monte_carlo_basics.qmd) | 30 min |
| +2:35 | [Sobol Sensitivity Analysis](../../library/sobol_sensitivity_analysis.qmd) | 25 min |
| +3:00 | **Break** | 15 min |
| +3:15 | [Bayesian Calibration Intro](../../library/bayesian_calibration_intro.qmd) | 60 min |
| | **Total** | **255 min** |

## Optional Extensions

If time permits:

- [Polynomial Chaos Expansion](../../library/polynomial_chaos_expansion.qmd) - For participants interested in surrogate modeling
- [Gaussian Process Surrogates](../../library/gaussian_process_surrogates.qmd) - Alternative surrogate with uncertainty quantification
- [Backend Alternatives](../../library/backend_alternatives.qmd) - For participants interested in PyTorch/GPU computing
- [Benchmark Setup](../../library/benchmark_setup.qmd) - For participants who want to create custom benchmarks

## Resources

- [GitHub Repository](https://github.com/sandialabs/pyapprox)
- [Documentation](https://sandialabs.github.io/pyapprox/)

## Instructor Notes

Detailed notes for each tutorial section:

### Setup Environment

Quick verification that everyone's environment is working.
Have attendees run the test code to confirm installation.
Address any conda/pip issues before proceeding.


### Uq Problem Framing

Key conceptual foundation. Emphasize the forward vs inverse problem distinction.
Use the Lotka-Volterra model as the running example throughout.
Draw the UQ workflow diagram on the board.


### Forward Model Definition

First hands-on coding. Introduce the benchmark interface.
Show how qoi_function() wraps the complexity of ODE solving.
Let attendees experiment with different parameter values.


### Random Variables Distributions

Connect probability concepts to UQ. Explain prior distributions.
Demonstrate sampling and histogram visualization.
Emphasize the role of the prior in Bayesian inference (foreshadowing).


### Quantities Of Interest

Discuss different QoI types and their practical interpretations.
Show how the same model can have different QoIs for different questions.
Connect to decision-making context.


### Monte Carlo Basics

Core UQ technique. Emphasize the 1/sqrt(N) convergence rate.
Have attendees predict how many samples they need for a given accuracy.
Discuss computational cost vs accuracy trade-off.


### Sobol Sensitivity Analysis

Practical sensitivity analysis. Explain first-order vs total-order indices.
Discuss implications: which parameters to measure more precisely?
Connect to experimental design decisions.


### Bayesian Calibration Intro

Capstone tutorial. Build up from Bayes' theorem to MCMC sampling.
Use a simple linear model first, then discuss applying to Lotka-Volterra.
Emphasize uncertainty quantification in the inferred parameters.
Leave time for the exercises if possible.
